raw_text: David Wilson-Okamura, ed. virgil.org. http://virgil.org The Perseus Project provides Vergil's Eclogues, Georgics, and Aeneid in English and Latin and the Aeneid commentaries of Servius (Latin only) and Conington-Nettleship-Haverfield. Latin texts can be displayed without links to the Morpheus forms analyser, and in the case of the epic, to the commentaries as well, English texts with links to the Perseus look-up tool. The Vergil Project is officially under construction until Fall 2000, but it is functional already. It offers (1) on-line tools for disambiguation of grammar, analysis of syntax, translation, and entry of discursive commentary for individual words, phrases, or longer sections of text. Access to these tools is available upon request to qualified collaborators working individually or in groups; (2) Text and Commentary. The site currently offers a Latin text of the Aeneid based on the editions of Mynors and Geymonat, grammatical and syntactical analysis, and glossary of Aeneid 1-2; and a concordance. By December 1999 these resources will have been supplemented with grammatical and syntactical analysis and glossary of Aeneid 4 and 6, and discursive commentary on books 1, 2, 4, and 6. Virgil.org maintains a web site devoted to Vergil and Vergiliana. It is the home of "Mantovano", a threaded discussion list devoted to "the works attributed to Publius Vergilius Maro," "Commentary on Virgil and his works from late antiquity .. to the present," and "Virgil's influence on subsequent literature." The website contains a form through which one can register for "Mantovano" as well as sections on bibliography, vitae, Augustus, (Julius) Caesar, translations, links, books, maps, etc. Vergilian Bibliography 1998-1999 81

raw_text: Walter Wimmel. "Vergils Tityrus und der perusinische Konflikt. Zum Verständnis der 1. Eclogue ," RhMus 141 (1998) 348-362. Harrison argues that Corydon is a "speaking name" (Gk. KopuBcóv "lark"). Ergo, "the Lark has been the lark for us", an acknowledgment of Corydon' s expert singing. Henderson weighs the main sectors of Eclogue 3, a mime about representation, a rumination on the spark of collaborative energy: Bickering (1-27), Bargaining (28-54), Balladeering (60-107), Balloting (55-59, 108-111), and Browsing: et quisquís (109). Papanghelis shows that Damoetas and Menalcas, from a Neoteric viewpoint, give contrasting but complementary aspects of the spirit and rhetoric of their literary circle. The exchanges of lines 84-91 are central and excite Palaemon's declaration of a draw. Literary self-consciousness is the ultimate test of the merits of the Vergilian herdsman-poet. Daphnis' judgment matches the fairness of Palaemon's: Corydon presented the better claim to superiority. Touratier examines different tenses in Eel. 7, 1-20. Wimmel provides a specific and plausible context for the first Eclogue. Vergilian Bibliography 1998-1999 83

Harrison argues that Corydon is a "speaking name" (Gk. KopuBcóv
"lark"). Ergo, "the Lark has been the lark for us", an acknow-
ledgment of Corydon' s expert singing. Henderson weighs the main
sectors of Eclogue 3, a mime about representation, a rumination on
the spark of collaborative energy: Bickering (1-27), Bargaining
(28-54), Balladeering (60-107), Balloting (55-59, 108-111), and
Browsing: et quisquís (109). Papanghelis shows that Damoetas and
Menalcas, from a Neoteric viewpoint, give contrasting but
complementary aspects of the spirit and rhetoric of their literary
circle. The exchanges of lines 84-91 are central and excite Palaemon's
declaration of a draw. Literary self-consciousness is the ultimate test
of the merits of the Vergilian herdsman-poet. Daphnis' judgment
matches the fairness of Palaemon's: Corydon presented the better
claim to superiority. Touratier examines different tenses in Eel. 7,
1-20. Wimmel provides a specific and plausible context for the first
Eclogue.

Schork detects three etymological allusions in the Georgics involving ancient Egyptian in a congenial wordplay: Georgics 4.208-121, 291; 3.28-29. Thomas regards the Georgics as a melding or integration of Hesiodic themes, maybe stylizations of agricultural folk elements, rustic Italian material, and new learned Alexandrian and Roman elements for modernity and new metaphorical meaning (213). "Songs of Hesiodic Greece, mediated through the refinement of Alexandrian Greece, would be appreciated by Vergil's audiences."

Adams and Mayer review the contents of the Proceedings of the British Academy volume 93 (1999) devoted to "Aspects of the Language of Latin Poetry" under separate rubrics: Neutral Terms, Prosaic Terms, Formal Speech or Prose, Colloquialism and Orality, Archaism, The Influence of Greek, Metaphor, and Word Order; Hyperbaton. Adams deals with the relationship between ordinary usage and certain "poetic" patterns. Coleman surveys the concepts of poetic diction, discourse, and register, including archaism and the debt to Greek, including the use of metaphor and specifically poetic syntax. Görler' s article bears on unexpected usages of transitive verbs which may amount to metaphor. Langslow deals with scientific language, Mayer with the influence of Greek and the use of technical vocabularies. Novara is concerned with tibicines: Aeneid 1.534, 7.258, 1.560. Penney 's focus is on syntactic features that derive from the oldest stratum of Latin usage.

raw_text: Jeffrey Wills. "Divided Allusion: Virgil and the Coma Berenices ." HSCP 98 (1998) 277-305. Balot refines and extends Wilkinson's Pindaric reading of the proem with specific, neglected parallels betwen Vergil and Octavian. Putnam analyzes Aeneid 12.896-900 for allusions, divine manipulation and the nature of heroism in the interplay of Homer and Vergil. "Aeneas' enraged slaying of his suppliant antagonist... runs blatantly counter to his father's ennobling dictum to spare the humbled foe (and) does not augur well for a sustained future, in his hands, of heroism as we know it from Homer and as Anchises movingly commands its moderation for Rome to come" (78). Wills studies Catullus 66.39 and Vergil, Aeneid 6.456 and questions why the imitation is so close, and seeks to determine the boundaries of the allusion.

raw_text: Sarah Spence. "Veiled allusions: Aeneas, Augustine, and Dante at Ostia." C&MA9 (1998) 143-161. Billanovich examines "Un libro del ragazzo Petrarca" (129-136); Venier studies "Additamenta danielini nel Virgilio di Petrarca" (137-147). Spence suggests that Augustine's conversation with his mother, Confess. 9.10. 23-25, may evoke the topos of the gates of horn and ivory. The gates gain importance and significance in mediaeval Christian literature: they mediate between life and the newly-determined hereafter.

raw_text: Richard Morton. "Bringing Virgil over into Britain: John Dry den Refigures Aeneid 1-5." Studies in Eighteenth Century Culture 27 (1998) 147-167. Morton argues that Dry den' s overt teaching imposed on his Aeneids may be his doctrines of exile, invasion, and usurpation or be the identification of Aeneas with William III. Morton's verdict is impressive: "...the power of his version relies in the consistently imposed moral vision: a voice committed to its ethical beliefs" (167).
